content.text: Based on the official Claude documentation and the coordinator-subagent pattern analysis,
"Subagents"
are
specialized, isolated worker agents spawned by a main coordinator agent
to handle specific tasks, each with their own context window and system prompt, reporting results back to the coordinator.
What It Is
Subagents are
focused workers that report back
to the main agent — they are spawned by the main agent, have isolated context windows and dedicated system prompts, and
only report results back to the main agent, never talking to each other
The Basic Flow

User Request
     ↓
Coordinator Agent
     ↓
  Route to specialist
     ↓
┌──────────────────────────────────┐
│ research_agent │ writer_agent │ ← Each: isolated context,
│ reviewer_agent │ any_specialist │ own system prompt, own tools
└──────────────────────────────────┘
     ↓
Tool result back to coordinator
     ↓
Coordinator synthesizes → Final output
Key Characteristics
Characteristic
What It Means
Isolated context
Each subagent has its own context window, separate from coordinator
Dedicated system prompt
Subagent gets specialized instructions for its role
One-way reporting
Subagents report back to coordinator only — no peer-to-peer communication
No shared state
Subagents share nothing; coordinator must explicitly pass context between them
Same loop mechanism
Uses same
stopReason
loop as single-agent; tool calls dispatch to separate API calls
How Subagents Work in Claude Code
Claude Code spawns subagents using the
Task tool

Claude Code Session
      ↓
Main agent → Task tool → specialist-1 (isolated context)
           → Task tool → specialist-2 (isolated context)
           → Task tool → specialist-3 (isolated context)
      ↓
All report back to main agent only — no sideways talk
      ↓
Main agent synthesizes output
Implementation pattern

// Launch parallel subagents (all in ONE message for true parallelism)
[Task]
description: "Security review"
prompt: "[detailed security checklist]"
run_in_background: true

[Task]
description: "Performance analysis"
prompt: "[detailed perf criteria]"
run_in_background: true

[Task]
description: "Test coverage review"
prompt: "[detailed test requirements]"
run_in_background: true
After launching, retrieve results:

TaskOutput: task_1_id
TaskOutput: task_2_id
TaskOutput: task_3_id
Why Use Subagents
Benefit
Explanation
Context management
Main agent stays focused while subagents handle deep work in isolated contexts
Parallel execution
Multiple subagents run simultaneously, reducing latency
Specialization
Each subagent has dedicated expertise via system prompt
Bottleneck avoidance
Main agent doesn't get bogged down in detailed execution
When to Use Subagents
Subagents are ideal when:
Condition
Why Subagents Help
Embarrassingly parallel tasks
Tasks don't need to share state during execution
Need specialized expertise
Each subagent gets role-specific system prompt
Main context getting too large
Subagents handle work in isolated contexts
Multiple independent analyses
Security review, perf analysis, test coverage can run concurrently
Fire-and-forget work
Tasks don't need mid-task peer communication
Good candidates

:
- Security review + performance analysis + test generation
- Multiple file refactoring (independent files)
- Research on different topics (parallel)
- Documentation updates across modules
When NOT to Use Subagents
Condition
Better Alternative
Tasks need peer-to-peer communication
Use Agent Teams (experimental)
Tasks have dependencies
Use sequential flow or orchestrator-workers
Cost is primary concern
Single agent is cheaper (subagents cost 3–4x)
Need mid-task information sharing
Use Agent Teams for peer communication
Production stability required
Use subagents (not Agent Teams which is experimental)
Subagents vs. Agent Teams
Aspect
Subagents
Agent Teams
Peer communication
No — one-way to coordinator only
Yes — direct peer-to-peer
Production status
✅ Stable & production-ready
⚠️ Experimental
Context
Isolated, fire-and-forget
Persistent, shared state
Token cost
3–4x single agent
3–4x+ (higher with peer talk)
Use when
Parallel tasks, no peer talk needed
Peers need mid-task sharing
The Critical Mistake: Context Isolation
Subagents share nothing
— if subagent B needs output from subagent A, coordinator must explicitly pass it:

# WRONG — writer has zero context
research = run_subagent("research_agent", task="research Lambda limits")
writer = run_subagent("writer_agent", task="write about Lambda")

# RIGHT — explicitly pass context through coordinator
research = run_subagent("research_agent", task="research Lambda limits")
writer = run_subagent("writer_agent", task="write about Lambda", context=research)
Isolation is the feature, not the bug
— design for explicit context passing.
Parallel Subagents
Multiple tool calls in one response run
simultaneously
, cutting latency:

// These 3 subagents run in parallel (same message)
[Task]
description: "Security review"
run_in_background: true

[Task]
description: "Performance analysis"  
run_in_background: true

[Task]
description: "Test coverage"
run_in_background: true
Parallel execution is
Nx faster where N = number of subagents
Token Cost Reality
Configuration
Token Cost
Single agent
1x
3 subagents
Agent Teams
3–4x+ (higher with peer communication)
Only use subagents when specialist quality justifies the 3–4x cost
Mental Model
Think of subagents like
specialized contractors working for a project manager

: the PM (coordinator) assigns tasks to electrical, plumbing, and HVAC contractors (subagents). Each contractor works in their own isolated space with their own tools, reports back to the PM when done, and never talks directly to other contractors. The PM must explicitly pass information between contractors if needed.
Key Takeaways
Subagents are specialized workers spawned by coordinator, with isolated context and dedicated system prompts
Subagents only report back to main agent — never talk to each other
Each subagent has its own context window, separate from coordinator
Subagents share nothing; coordinator must explicitly pass context between them
In Claude Code: use Task tool with
run_in_background: true
for parallel execution
Parallel subagents (same message) run simultaneously, cutting latency
Cost: 3–4x single agent — only use when specialist quality justifies it
Production-ready and stable (unlike Agent Teams which is experimental)
Good for: embarrassingly parallel tasks, specialized expertise, context management
Bad for: tasks needing peer-to-peer communication, dependencies, cost-sensitive work
Subagents follow the coordinator-subagent pattern: coordinator receives task, delegates to specialists via tool calls, subagents report back, coordinator synthesizes.
How It Works in Practice
Understanding the internals of subagents helps you predict its behavior and debug issues effectively.

When a request is processed, several layers of the system interact: the client library validates inputs and manages retries, the transport layer handles HTTP/2 connection multiplexing, and the API server routes to the appropriate model instance. Each layer introduces latency that you can measure and optimize.

For production deployments, understanding this pipeline helps you allocate timeout budgets appropriately. The network round-trip typically adds 50-100ms, while model processing time scales with output length. A response generating 1,000 tokens takes 2-4x longer than one generating 250 tokens for the same model.
Best Practices
Production experience with subagents has surfaced these practices that consistently improve reliability and performance.
Use async clients for concurrent workloads — Python's AsyncAnthropic and TypeScript's async/await patterns avoid blocking the event loop during API calls.
Cache system prompts with cache_control — stable system prompts hit the cache ~90% of the time after the first call, reducing both latency and cost significantly.
Stream responses for interactive use — streaming reduces time-to-first-token from seconds to milliseconds, dramatically improving perceived responsiveness.
Implement structured retry logic — use exponential backoff starting at 1 second, with jitter to prevent thundering herd problems under rate limiting.
Log structured metadata — capture model, input_tokens, output_tokens, latency, and request_id in every log entry for effective debugging and cost tracking.
Set explicit max_tokens — always specify max_tokens to bound cost and prevent unexpectedly long responses that consume your token budget.
Common Pitfalls
Awareness of these common failure modes prevents hours of debugging in production.
Not validating response structure — even when you request JSON, parse it with a try/catch. Models occasionally produce malformed output under high load or edge-case inputs.
Accumulating conversation history without truncation — after 10-20 turns, conversations can exceed context windows. Implement a sliding window or summarization strategy.
Mixing prompt concerns — put behavior instructions in system prompts, task-specific content in user turns. Mixing them reduces clarity and makes iteration harder.
Ignoring stop_reason — check if stop_reason is "max_tokens" rather than "end_turn". Truncated responses need handling differently than complete ones.
Creating new client instances per request — instantiate the Anthropic client once at module load time to benefit from connection pooling across requests.
Performance Benchmarks and Cost Analysis
Making informed architectural decisions requires concrete numbers. These benchmarks are representative of typical production workloads.
Claude Haiku 3.5: ~$0.80/MTok input, ~$4/MTok output. Best for high-volume, simple tasks like classification, extraction, and summarization.
Claude Sonnet 4: ~$3/MTok input, ~$15/MTok output. The best quality-to-cost ratio for most production use cases requiring reasoning.
Prompt cache hit rate: After the first call, 85-95% of requests hit the cache for stable prompts. Cache tokens cost 10% of normal input token price.
Message Batches API: 50% cost reduction for async workloads processed within 24 hours. Ideal for nightly data processing, evaluation runs, and bulk content generation.
Typical latency: 1-3s for Haiku, 3-10s for Sonnet, 10-30s for Opus. Streaming makes all models feel 2-5x faster from the user's perspective.
Security Considerations
Deploying subagents in production requires careful attention to security boundaries and trust models.
API key management — store keys in environment variables or secrets managers (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault). Never commit to version control.
Prompt injection prevention — when embedding user input in prompts, use XML tags to clearly delimit user content from instructions, reducing injection attack surface.
Output sanitization — before rendering AI-generated content in web UIs, escape HTML to prevent XSS attacks from model-produced content.
Rate limiting user requests — implement per-user rate limits in addition to API limits to prevent cost abuse and ensure fair service distribution.
Summary

Subagents is a foundational capability for building production-ready Claude integrations. By following the patterns in this guide — proper error handling, async clients, prompt caching, and structured monitoring — you can build reliable, cost-efficient applications that scale gracefully from prototype to production.

Summary

Subagents is a foundational capability for building production-ready Claude integrations. By following the patterns in this guide — proper error handling, async clients, prompt caching, and structured monitoring — you can build reliable, cost-efficient applications that scale gracefully from prototype to production.
Designing Effective Subagent Prompts

The system prompt for each subagent is the primary mechanism for specialization, and it is worth investing significant engineering effort in getting it right. Unlike a generic assistant prompt, a subagent system prompt should define a highly constrained role with explicit scope boundaries: what the agent is responsible for, what it is explicitly not responsible for, the exact format of its output, and how it should handle ambiguous or out-of-scope input. Scope clarity prevents scope creep — if a research subagent's prompt does not explicitly state that it should not attempt to write prose or make editorial judgments, it may do so opportunistically, producing output that conflicts with the writer subagent's work.

Output format specification in subagent prompts is especially important because the coordinator agent will programmatically parse or summarize each subagent's response. If the research subagent sometimes returns a bulleted list and sometimes returns a JSON object depending on the task, the coordinator must handle both formats or failures will cascade. The most robust approach is to require structured output from every subagent — either JSON with a fixed schema, or a human-readable format with clearly labeled sections that the coordinator can parse with simple regex or string matching. Include explicit examples of the expected output format in the system prompt itself, not just in the initial user message, so the format expectation is always in scope.
Open every subagent system prompt with a single-sentence role definition: 'You are a security reviewer whose only job is to identify security vulnerabilities in the provided code.'
Explicitly state what is out of scope to prevent scope creep: 'Do not suggest refactoring, performance improvements, or feature additions.'
Specify the exact output schema with a worked example embedded in the system prompt.
Include handling instructions for edge cases: 'If no security issues are found, return an empty findings array — never say there is nothing to report.'
Define confidence levels for findings so the coordinator can weight results appropriately: high/medium/low confidence.
Calibrate token budgets per subagent: a classification subagent might need only 128 output tokens, while a summarization subagent might need 1024.
Version subagent system prompts explicitly and store them in source control alongside the coordinator logic.
Test each subagent independently with a suite of representative inputs before wiring it into the coordinator pipeline.

# Well-structured subagent system prompt with schema and examples
SECURITY_REVIEWER_PROMPT = """
You are a security reviewer. Your ONLY job is to identify security vulnerabilities
in the provided code. Do NOT suggest refactoring, performance improvements, or
style changes -- those are out of scope.

Output a JSON object with this exact schema:
{
  "findings": [
    {
      "severity": "critical" | "high" | "medium" | "low",
      "category": "injection" | "auth" | "secrets" | "input_validation" | "other",
      "line_numbers": [<int>, ...],
      "description": "<one sentence describing the issue>",
      "recommendation": "<one sentence fix>"
    }
  ],
  "overall_risk": "critical" | "high" | "medium" | "low" | "none"
}

If no security issues are found, return: {"findings": [], "overall_risk": "none"}
"""

import anthropic, json

async def run_security_review(code: str, client: anthropic.AsyncAnthropic) -> dict:
    response = await client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5",
        max_tokens=1024,
        system=SECURITY_REVIEWER_PROMPT,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": f"Review this code:\n\n```\n{code}\n```"}]
    )
    raw = response.content[0].text.strip()
    return json.loads(raw)
Result Aggregation Patterns

The coordinator agent's job after collecting subagent results is aggregation: combining multiple specialized outputs into a single coherent final result. The right aggregation strategy depends heavily on the nature of the subagent outputs. For parallel independent analyses such as security review, performance analysis, and code quality — where each subagent examines the same artifact from a different angle — the coordinator typically synthesizes findings into a unified report with conflict resolution for overlapping concerns. For pipeline subagents where each agent hands off to the next — research feeds writer feeds reviewer — the coordinator's role is more of an orchestration loop than true aggregation.

When subagents produce structured output (JSON with defined schemas), aggregation can be done deterministically without another LLM call, which saves tokens and latency. For example, if three independent fact-checking subagents each return a list of verified and unverified claims, the coordinator can compute the intersection and union of their results programmatically and only invoke an LLM call to resolve the genuinely ambiguous cases. This hybrid approach — deterministic aggregation for clear-cut cases, LLM synthesis for ambiguous ones — provides better cost and latency characteristics than routing all aggregation through a single coordinator LLM call.
Use deterministic aggregation (code logic) for structured JSON outputs wherever possible — reserve LLM synthesis for unstructured or ambiguous cases.
Define a clear conflict resolution policy before implementing aggregation: does the highest severity finding win, or do you show all findings ranked by severity?
For voting-based aggregation (majority wins), require an odd number of subagents to avoid ties.
Preserve provenance in aggregated results: indicate which subagent produced each finding for debugging and audit trails.
Implement a schema validation step after each subagent returns before aggregation to catch malformed outputs early.
For quality-sensitive aggregation, track which subagents' outputs were discarded or overridden so you can audit coordinator reasoning.
Consider a dedicated aggregation subagent for complex synthesis tasks — the coordinator then becomes a pure orchestrator, not also a synthesizer.
